skill.name: siteground-cache
skill.description: Bypass SiteGround, LiteSpeed, and browser caching for WordPress staging development by adding admin-only cache-busting code to a child theme.
skill.summary: Adds and removes WordPress staging cache-busting helpers for SiteGround development workflows.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Most static external command detections in SKILL.md are documentation code fences, and the shell substitutions in the scripts are mostly quoted utility use. The confirmed risk is high because the skill asks an AI agent to collect and store FTP credentials, then documents FTP deployment with certificate verification disabled.

high_findings.title: FTP Credential Collection and Storage Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to request FTP host, username, password, and port, then store credentials in CLAUDE.local.md. This creates a high-risk secret handling workflow even though no exfiltration code was found.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly asks for FTP passwords and provides a credentials template. The stated gitignored storage reduces accidental commits, but it still exposes secrets to the agent and local files.

high_findings.title: Insecure FTP Deployment Example
high_findings.description: The documented deployment command disables SSL certificate verification and uses an ftp:// endpoint. This can expose credentials and uploaded files to interception or server impersonation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deployment block explicitly shows certificate verification disabled and an FTP URL. This is a clear transport security issue in a credential-bearing workflow.

medium_findings.title: Documentation-Only Production Safeguard
medium_findings.description: The skill warns against production writes, but the installer and remover scripts accept any theme path and do not verify that the target is staging. A user or agent could modify a production functions.php file by mistake.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The warning language is strong, but enforcement is absent in the shell scripts. The mismatch creates a realistic accidental production-change risk.

medium_findings.title: Direct Modification of WordPress Theme Files
medium_findings.description: The installer appends PHP code to functions.php and the remover rewrites functions.php through a temporary file. Backups are created, but these scripts still mutate application code based on a provided path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is intentional and documented, but it can alter live application behavior. Quoting and backups reduce command injection and recovery risk.

medium_findings.title: Cache Bypass Code Can Harm Production Performance
medium_findings.description: The PHP snippet disables LiteSpeed and SG CachePress caching for administrators and adds changing timestamps to theme assets. If left on a production site, it can degrade performance and hide real caching behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code intentionally disables caching and appends time-based query strings. It is useful for staging, but harmful if retained outside the intended context.

low_findings.title: Shell Substitution Findings Are Mostly Benign Utility Use
low_findings.description: The shell substitutions in the scripts resolve the script directory, create timestamped backup names, and read a local snippet file. The relevant variables are quoted, and no evidence of attacker-controlled command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is real, but the commands are fixed utilities and not constructed from untrusted command text. The remaining risk is operational file modification, captured in medium findings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the description metadata, which contains no cryptographic algorithm or hashing code. No evidence of weak cryptography was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is plain descriptive text about cache bypassing. There is no crypto API, digest algorithm, or password hashing behavior at that location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Disabled Certificate Verification With FTP Deployment
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment example combines disabled certificate verification with an FTP URL in a workflow that uses passwords. This is unsafe for marketplace publication without a warning or safer SFTP-only guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous options are explicitly present in the documented deployment block. The risk follows directly from the credential-bearing network workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Managed Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the user to provide and locally store FTP passwords for future sessions. This increases the blast radius of any prompt injection, logging, or local file disclosure elsewhere in a project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credentials workflow is explicit and central to the deployment instructions. No direct exfiltration was found, so the concern is exposure and misuse risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Production Target Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts modify the provided theme path without checking whether the path belongs to staging. This makes the staging-only policy dependent on user and agent discipline.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts take the path argument directly and then modify functions.php beneath it. The documentation says production is blocked, but the scripts do not enforce that rule.

content.user_title: Bypass SiteGround Cache for WordPress Staging
content.value_statement: WordPress changes can be hidden by SiteGround and browser caching during staging reviews. This skill adds admin-only cache bypassing and removal scripts for child themes.
actual_capabilities: Adds a SiteGround cache-busting PHP snippet to a WordPress child theme functions.php file.
Creates a timestamped backup before modifying functions.php.
Removes the inserted cache-busting functions from functions.php.
Adds admin-only no-cache headers for LiteSpeed Cache and SG CachePress workflows.
Adds changing version parameters to theme CSS and JavaScript URLs for administrators.
Documents a staging-only deployment and verification workflow.
limitations: Requires a WordPress child theme with an existing functions.php file.
Does not programmatically verify that a target path belongs to staging.
Requires administrator login to see the banner and asset cache busting.
FTP credential handling must be managed carefully outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Verify Theme Changes on Staging
use_cases.description: Add temporary cache bypassing so visual and asset changes appear immediately for WordPress administrators.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Client Review Site
use_cases.description: Enable a visible admin banner and fresh CSS or JavaScript loading before a staging review.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Before Release
use_cases.description: Remove the development cache-busting functions after staging verification and before production deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Add Cache Busting
prompt_templates.prompt: Add SiteGround cache busting to this WordPress child theme on staging. Create a backup and tell me what changed.

prompt_templates.title: Check Existing Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this child theme and tell me whether the SiteGround cache buster is already installed.

prompt_templates.title: Remove Development Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the SiteGround cache-busting code from this staging child theme and confirm the backup location.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Staging Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staging cache-busting workflow for SiteGround and identify any production, credential, or deployment risks.

output_examples.input: Add cache busting to my staging child theme.
output_examples.output: The theme backup was created, cache-busting functions were appended, and the staging site should show an admin-only banner after upload.

output_examples.input: Why are my CSS changes still not visible?
output: Confirm you are logged in as a WordPress administrator.
Check that the child theme is active.
Verify that theme asset URLs include a changing version parameter.
Purge SiteGround and any CDN cache if stale content remains.

output_examples.output: The development cache-busting functions should be removed, the backup retained, and production deployment should be completed manually by the user.

best_practices: Use this skill only on staging or local WordPress environments.
Remove the cache-busting code before production release.
Use secure credential handling and prefer SFTP or key-based deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not run the installer against a production theme path.
Do not commit FTP passwords or local credential notes to source control.
Do not disable certificate verification for real deployments.

faq.question: Does this skill support production deployment?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for staging and local development workflows only.

faq.question: What WordPress file does it modify?
faq.answer: It modifies the child theme functions.php file and creates a timestamped backup first.

faq.question: Who sees the development banner?
faq.answer: Only logged-in WordPress users with administrator capability should see the banner.

faq.question: Does it bypass caching for all visitors?
faq.answer: No. The PHP logic targets administrators, but production use is still discouraged.

faq.question: Can it remove the changes later?
faq.answer: Yes. The removal script is designed to remove the inserted cache-busting functions and related comments.

faq.question: Why is the security rating high?
faq.answer: The skill includes FTP credential handling and an insecure FTP deployment example with certificate verification disabled.
